e_visa.notes: Thailand e-Visa system (thaievisa.go.th) launched globally on 1 January 2025 across 94 Royal Thai Embassies and Consulates-General. Applies to nationalities not entitled to visa exemption. Specific visa types and stay durations depend on visa category applied for.

visa_policy.notes: IMPORTANT - POLICY IN TRANSITION (as of June 27, 2026): On 19 May 2026, Thailand's Cabinet approved a major revision that: (1) revokes the 60-day visa exemption for all 93 countries (active since 15 July 2024); (2) introduces a new 30-day visa exemption for 54 countries/territories; (3) introduces a new 15-day visa exemption for 3 countries (Maldives, Mauritius, Seychelles); (4) reduces Visa on Arrival to only 4 countries (Azerbaijan, Belarus, India, Serbia); (5) maintains bilateral 30-day agreements for China, Hong Kong SAR, Kazakhstan, Laos, Macau SAR, Mongolia, Russia, Timor-Leste, and Vietnam; (6) maintains bilateral 90-day agreements for Argentina, Brazil, Chile, Peru, and South Korea. As of June 27, 2026, the Royal Gazette publication has NOT yet occurred, so ALL entries in this file reflect the CURRENT LEGALLY ACTIVE SITUATION (60-day general exemption / 31-country VoA) with PENDING CHANGE notes. The 54-country 30-day list (from multiple secondary sources): Australia, Austria, Bahrain, Belgium, Bhutan, Brunei, Canada, Czech Republic, Denmark, Estonia, Fiji, Finland, France, Georgia, Germany, Greece, Hungary, Iceland, Indonesia, Ireland, Israel, Italy, Japan, Jordan, Kuwait, Kyrgyzstan, Latvia, Liechtenstein, Lithuania, Luxembourg, Malaysia, Netherlands, New Zealand, Norway, Oman, Philippines, Poland, Portugal, Qatar, Romania, Saudi Arabia, Singapore, Slovakia, South Africa, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, Taiwan, Turkey, UAE, UK, Ukraine, USA, and likely some additional countries (exact list unconfirmed from official .go.th source). Cambodia and Myanmar bilateral 14-day exemptions are unaffected. Current extensible one 30-day extension at immigration discretion applies for visa-exempt entrants.

visa_types.name: TR Visa (Tourist)
visa_types.notes: Applied at Thai embassy/consulate. Single-entry (TR) or multiple-entry (TRMT) available. Standard single-entry TR: 60 days, extendable once by 30 days at immigration office (THB 1,900 fee). Multiple-entry TR valid 6 months, each stay up to 60 days.

visa_types.name: Thai e-Visa
visa_types.purpose: Tourism (and other visa categories via online application)
visa_types.notes: Thai e-Visa system launched globally January 1, 2025 across 94 Thai embassies/consulates. Applies to nationalities not entitled to visa exemption. Online application via thaievisa.go.th. Multiple visa types (TR, Non-B, Non-O, etc.) available through the portal. Specific stay durations depend on visa category.

visa_types.name: Non-Immigrant O-A Visa (Retirement)
visa_types.purpose: Retirement in Thailand for persons aged 50+
visa_types.notes: For applicants aged 50+. Requires proof of funds: THB 800,000 in Thai bank account (or monthly income/pension of THB 65,000, or combination totaling THB 800,000). Must have health insurance with minimum THB 40,000 outpatient / THB 400,000 inpatient coverage. 1-year stay, extendable annually at immigration. Applied at Thai embassy/consulate abroad.

visa_types.name: Non-Immigrant B Visa (Business/Work)
visa_types.purpose: Business activities or employment in Thailand
visa_types.notes: For business activities or employment. Requires letter from Thai employer or business. Initial 90-day stay; must obtain work permit and convert to 1-year extension of stay at immigration in Thailand. Multiple-entry Non-B available for established employees.

visa_types.name: Long-Term Resident Visa - Work-From-Thailand (LTR-WFT)
visa_types.purpose: Remote work from Thailand for foreign employer
visa_types.notes: 10-year LTR visa for Work-From-Thailand Professionals. Requirements: employed by a publicly listed or large foreign company; minimum personal income USD 80,000/year (or USD 40,000/year with master's degree/investment/work in targeted field); health insurance USD 50,000+ coverage. 5-year permission to stay, renewable once for additional 5 years. THB 50,000 visa fee (~USD 1,430). 17% flat income tax option available. No work permit needed for remote/overseas employer income. Issued by Thailand BOI.

visa_on_arrival.name: Bahrain
visa_on_arrival.note: ordinary passport; at intl airports & King Fahd Causeway only

outbound_official.notes: Source: Thai Ministry of Foreign Affairs, Department of Consular Affairs official PDF dated 12 May 2026 (พ.ศ. 2569), titled 'List of Countries and Territories where Thai Nationals may enter without a visa/visa exemption' and 'Summary of Countries and Territories entitled to Visa on Arrival (VOA)'. * = unilateral (destination grants exemption to Thailand only); non-asterisked = bilateral agreement. Numbers in parentheses = maximum stay in days for ordinary passport holders. Some VOA entries cover only diplomatic/official passports as noted.

cbi.notes: Thailand does not offer a Citizenship by Investment program. No official government source confirms any such program. Naturalization requires 10 consecutive years of permanent residency, Thai language proficiency, ability to sing the national anthem, and meeting income requirements.

programs.name: Long-Term Resident (LTR) Visa
programs.notes: 10-year renewable visa (5-year initial permission to stay, extendable another 5 years). Four categories: (1) Wealthy Global Citizens - min USD 1 million in global assets + USD 500,000 investment in Thailand (government bonds, company stock, or property); (2) Wealthy Pensioners - age 50+, income USD 80,000/year or USD 40,000/year + USD 250,000 Thai investment; (3) Work-from-Thailand Professionals - min income USD 80,000/year, employed by overseas company; (4) Highly-Skilled Professionals - min income USD 80,000/year, working in targeted industries. Visa issuance fee: THB 50,000 per person per 10 years. Processing: qualification endorsement ~20 working days. Benefits include 17% flat personal income tax (category 4), exemption from 4-Thai-to-1-foreigner employment ratio, 1-year reporting cycle, fast-track airport service. LTR amounts confirmed via ltr.boi.go.th (official BOI domain).

programs.name: Thailand Privilege Card (formerly Thailand Elite Visa)
programs.notes: State enterprise program operated by Thailand Privilege Card Co., Ltd. (sole shareholder: Tourism Authority of Thailand). Rebranded from Thailand Elite Visa in October 2023. Multiple membership tiers: Bronze (THB 650,000, 5-year visa, limited period offer), Gold (THB 900,000, 5-year visa with Privilege Points), Diamond, Reserve. Multiple-entry visa granting residency rights for 5–20+ years depending on tier. No income or investment proof required. One-time fee. No path to permanent residency or citizenship through this program.

programs.name: SMART Visa
programs.eligibility: Five categories: SMART-T (Talent/highly-skilled experts in targeted industries, min income THB 100,000/month); SMART-E (Senior Executives, min income THB 200,000/month, 10+ years experience, bachelor's degree, senior management role); SMART-I (Investors in targeted industries); SMART-S (Startup entrepreneurs, THB 600,000 min deposit held 3+ months, company director or 25%+ equity in Thai firm); SMART-O (Dependents of SMART visa holders). All must work in 18 targeted S-Curve industries. Digital nomads/remote workers without Thai employment contracts ineligible.
programs.notes: Maximum 4-year renewable permission to stay (2 years for SMART-S startup employees). No work permit required for endorsed employers. Spouse and dependents receive equal stay permission; spouse may work without separate permit. 1-year reporting cycle. No re-entry permit needed. Fast-track airport services available.

programs.name: Destination Thailand Visa (DTV)
programs.eligibility: Foreign nationals working remotely for overseas employers, freelancers, digital content creators. Requirements: passport valid 6+ months, bank statements (3 months) with min ending balance THB 500,000, proof of income/salary slips (6 months), foreign employment contract or certificate authenticated by embassy.
programs.processing_time: 2 working days from complete document submission
programs.notes: 5-year multiple-entry visa. Each entry permits stay up to 180 days, extendable once for additional 180 days (total 180 days per entry). After maximum stay reached, must depart and re-enter using same DTV. Issued by Thai embassies/consulates abroad. MFA official program.

agreements: ASEAN Framework Agreement on Visa Exemption (bilateral visa exemption agreements with all ASEAN member states)

conditional_access.conditions: Bilateral Agreement on Visa Exemption for Diplomatic/Official Passports. Ordinary passport holders of Bangladesh are not in the visa-exempt or VoA lists and must obtain a visa. Listed in MFA Department of Consular Affairs document dated 12 November 2025, 30-day column.

Bilateral Agreement on Visa Exemption for Diplomatic/Official Passports. Ordinary passport holders must obtain a visa. Listed in MFA Department of Consular Affairs document dated 12 November 2025, 30-day column.

Bilateral Agreement on Visa Exemption for Diplomatic/Official Passports. Ordinary passport holders must obtain a visa. Listed in MFA Department of Consular Affairs document dated 12 November 2025, 30-day column.

Bilateral Agreement for diplomatic passport holders ONLY - the PDF explicitly notes 'Diplomatic pp. only', excluding official/service passport holders. Ordinary passport holders must obtain a visa. Listed in MFA Department of Consular Affairs document dated 12 November 2025, 30-day column.

Bilateral Agreement on Visa Exemption for Diplomatic/Official Passports. Ordinary passport holders must obtain a visa. Listed in MFA Department of Consular Affairs document dated 12 November 2025, 90-day column.

Bilateral Agreement on Visa Exemption for Diplomatic/Official Passports. Ordinary passport holders must obtain a visa. Listed in MFA Department of Consular Affairs document dated 12 November 2025, 90-day column.

Bilateral Agreement on Visa Exemption for Diplomatic/Official Passports. Ordinary passport holders must obtain a visa. Listed in MFA Department of Consular Affairs document dated 12 November 2025, 90-day column.

Bilateral Agreement on Visa Exemption for Diplomatic/Official Passports. Ordinary passport holders are not in the 93-country visa-exempt list; status under VoA uncertain (see gaps). Listed in MFA Department of Consular Affairs document dated 12 November 2025, 90-day column.

validation_notes: VALIDATED June 14 2026 against official Thai government sources (PRD.go.th, MFA.go.th, Doha Thai Embassy, TAT Newsroom, GulfNews).

CORRECTION 1 (Brazil max_stay_days): Brazil was listed at 60 days in visa_free; corrected to 90 days - Brazil holds a bilateral 90-day agreement independent of the general exemption scheme, confirmed by official MFA bilateral list and by the Cabinet May 2026 statement that bilateral agreements are unchanged.

CORRECTION 2 (South Korea max_stay_days): South Korea was listed at 60 days with a note saying 'may grant 90 days'; corrected to 90 days as the definitive bilateral agreement - confirmed by MFA bilateral list and Cabinet May 2026 confirmation of unchanged bilaterals.

CORRECTION 3 (Peru max_stay_days): Peru was listed at 60 days; corrected to 90 days under bilateral agreement - consistent with Argentina and Chile classification and confirmed by the Cabinet's May 2026 statement.

CORRECTION 4 (Bhutan duplicate): Bhutan appeared in both visa_free (60 days) and visa_on_arrival (15 days). The Doha page listed it in both categories which was contradictory. Bhutan is classified under the 60-day visa-exempt list (ordinary passport) per the July 2024 expansion. The duplicate VoA entry has been removed.

CORRECTION 5 (India reclassification added): India's imminent move from visa_free (60 days) to visa_on_arrival is now documented - added India entry to visa_on_arrival with PENDING status, and added PENDING note to visa_free India entry.

CORRECTION 6 (Azerbaijan added as new VoA country): Azerbaijan was not in the prior record at all. Cabinet decision of 19 May 2026 adds Azerbaijan to the new 4-country VoA list. Added with PENDING status.

CORRECTION 7 (Cabinet decision of 19 May 2026 fully documented): Prior record had a brief mention in the notes field only. All individual country entries now carry PENDING CHANGE notes classifying them correctly under the transition. The policy_notes section has been completely rewritten to document the transition clearly.
CORRECTION 8 (Kyrgyzstan reclassification noted): Kyrgyzstan moves from VoA to the 30-day visa exemption category in the new framework. Its VoA entry now notes this pending upgrade.

CORRECTION 9 (Maldives, Mauritius, Seychelles reclassification): All three were at 60 days exempt (Maldives/Mauritius) or 15-day VoA (Seychelles). Under new framework all three get 15-day visa exemption (free, no fee). Notes updated accordingly.

UNCHANGED: LTR Visa min_amount (USD 500,000) confirmed from ltr.boi.go.th. SMART Visa details unchanged. DTV details unchanged. CBI remains false. Diplomatic/official passport conditional_access entries unchanged. Bilateral 14-day ASEAN (Cambodia, Myanmar) unchanged.

VALIDATED June 27 2026 (re-validation): Official sources re-visited including PRD.go.th, MFA.go.th press briefing 19 May 2026, TAT Newsroom, multiple legal/travel sources. FINDING: Royal Gazette has still NOT been published as of June 27, 2026. The 60-day scheme remains the currently active legal regime at Thai borders. CORRECTION 10 (bilateral agreements for China, HKG, Russia, Vietnam, Mongolia, Kazakhstan): Previous PENDING notes stated these countries would be 'removed' from visa exemption. This was inaccurate - they have confirmed bilateral agreements (30-day) that will remain operative after the Royal Gazette publishes. Their notes have been updated to reflect the transition from general scheme to bilateral scheme rather than outright removal. Confirmed bilateral countries in new framework (secondary sources, consistent with MFA outbound bilateral PDF): China, Hong Kong SAR, Kazakhstan, Laos, Macau SAR, Mongolia, Russia, Timor-Leste, Vietnam. CORRECTION 11 (visa_policy notes field): Updated 'as of June 14' to 'as of June 27, 2026' and documented the bilateral 30-day group and the unconfirmed 54-country composition.

VALIDATED June 29 2026 (re-validation): Official sources re-visited: thailand.prd.go.th (Cabinet announcement page), mfa.go.th (MFA press briefing, general info), consular.mfa.go.th (Thai-language consular announcement), tatnews.org (TAT Newsroom), doha.thaiembassy.org (embassy visa page, last updated May 24 2026). FINDING: Royal Gazette has STILL NOT been published as of June 29, 2026. The Doha Thai Embassy page (official .thaiembassy.org domain, last updated May 24, 2026) continues to show the 60-day exemption and 31-country VoA scheme as currently active. No corrections to individual country records are required. All PENDING CHANGE notes in this file remain accurate. The 60-day general scheme is the legally enforced policy at Thai borders as of this validation date. No new official source identified any changes to bilateral agreements, cbi, rbi, or fast_track data.
gaps: Royal Gazette publication date for the Cabinet decision of 19 May 2026 is still not known as of June 29, 2026 (re-confirmed by re-visiting thailand.prd.go.th, mfa.go.th, doha.thaiembassy.org, and multiple secondary sources). All PENDING CHANGE notes will need updating once published. The effective date will be 15 days after Royal Gazette publication. The Doha embassy page (last updated May 24, 2026) still shows the 60-day / 31-VoA scheme as currently active.

The exact 54-country list for the new 30-day visa exemption scheme has not been published on any official Thai .go.th source. The composition cited in this file's notes field is from multiple secondary travel/legal sources and is likely accurate but must be re-verified against the Royal Gazette or official MFA announcement when published.

The exact list of countries removed from VoA (the 27 that were in 31-country VoA but not in new 4-country VoA) is confirmed indirectly from media reports, not from an official Thai government page listing the new policy.

The official MFA PDF documents listing all 93 visa-exempt and 31 VoA countries are image-based PDFs and could not be text-extracted; the country list was sourced from the Royal Thai Embassy Doha HTML page (official domain doha.thaiembassy.org).
The Thailand Privilege Card official domain tpc.go.th was unreachable during research; membership fees cited are from the thailandprivilege.co.th domain (state enterprise site) and secondary sources.
Exact countries eligible for e-Visa application through thaievisa.go.th not enumerated - the system went global January 2025 but specific country-level access via individual embassies varies.

Tunisia ordinary passport status under the new framework is unclear - Tunisia was not in the 93-country exempt group and the prior VoA status was already uncertain. Needs verification once Royal Gazette publishes.

Albania, Andorra, Colombia, Croatia, Cuba, Cyprus, Dominica, Dominican Republic, Ecuador, Guatemala, Jamaica, Kosovo, Malta, Mexico, Monaco, Morocco, Panama, Papua New Guinea, San Marino, Serbia, Slovenia, Sri Lanka, Tonga, Trinidad and Tobago, Uruguay, Uzbekistan - these are listed in the current file as 'removed from exemption.' Secondary sources suggest some (e.g., Croatia, Cyprus, Malta, Monaco, San Marino, Andorra, Slovenia) may actually be in the new 54-country 30-day group. Cannot confirm from official .go.th source as of June 27, 2026 - must be re-verified when Royal Gazette publishes.

Laos, Macau SAR, and Timor-Leste are confirmed to have bilateral 30-day agreements with Thailand under the new framework (from multiple secondary sources consistent with MFA bilateral documentation), but they are not yet reflected as separate visa_free entries in this file since their current status under the 93-country scheme or separate bilateral has not been individually confirmed from an official HTML page.

No credential-based conditional entry rules (entry based on holding a valid US visa, Schengen visa, UK visa, residence permit, Green Card) were found on any official Thai government source. Thailand's entry policy is based exclusively on nationality/passport type.
SMART-I (Investor) minimum investment amounts not confirmed from official source - minimum investment thresholds for SMART-I category not clearly stated on official pages.
